section.title: In God I Trust, I Shall Not Fear
section.sectionRationale: Written when the Philistines seized David in Gath, 1 Samuel 21:10-15, a moment of acute physical danger in hostile territory. The psalm's refrain, in God I trust, I shall not be afraid, what can man do to me, is quoted directly in Hebrews 13:6, applying David's confidence to every believer.
section.thesis: Seized by his enemies in Gath, David turns fear into trust with a settled refrain, in God I trust, I shall not be afraid, confident that the God who counts every tear also holds his life, a confidence the writer of Hebrews later applies directly to every believer.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the plea for mercy amid relentless pursuit and the refrain of trust; the enemies' scheming exposed and God's tender, intimate care; and the closing refrain repeated with a vow of thanksgiving and walking in the light of life.

units.label: Be merciful to me, O God
units.note: the refrain of trust amid pursuit

verses.text:
Be merciful to me, O God, for men are hounding me; 
all day they press their attack.
My enemies pursue me all day long, for many proudly assail me.
When I am afraid, I put my {{batach|trust}} in You.
In God, whose word I praise, in God I trust. I will not be afraid. What can man do to me?

units.label: You have taken account of my wanderings
units.note: the enemies' scheming and God's intimate care

verses.text: All day long they twist my words; all their thoughts are on my demise.

They conspire, they lurk, they watch my steps 
while they wait to take my life.
In spite of such sin, will they escape? In Your anger, O God, cast down the nations.

You have taken account of my wanderings. Put my tears in Your {{nod|bottle}}, 
are they not in Your book?

Then my enemies will retreat on the day I cry for help. 
By this I will know that God is on my side.

units.label: In God I trust, I will not be afraid
units.note: the refrain repeated, walking in the light of life

verses.text: In God, whose word I praise, in the LORD, whose word I praise,
in God I trust; I will not be afraid. What can man do to me?
Your vows are upon me, O God; I will render thank offerings to You.
For You have delivered my soul from death, and my feet from stumbling, that I may walk before God in the light of life.

batach.gloss: To trust, to feel secure, to rely upon. This verb anchors the psalm's refrain, repeated in verses 3, 4, and 11, describing a deliberate, repeated act of the will, choosing to lean the full weight of one's fear onto God rather than onto circumstances.

nod.gloss: Bottle or wineskin. David pictures God so intimately attentive to his suffering that He gathers every tear into a bottle and records it in a book, an image of God's detailed, personal knowledge of and care for the sufferer.

basic.context: This psalm arises from one of David's most physically vulnerable moments, seized by the Philistines in Gath while fleeing Saul, 1 Samuel 21:10-15. Surrounded by enemies who twist his words and plot his death, David repeats a settled refrain three times: in God I trust, I shall not be afraid, what can man do to me. He pictures God so attentive to his suffering that He collects every tear and records every wandering step in a book.
basic.hermeneutics: The refrain in this psalm is not a denial of real danger but a deliberate choice made in the midst of it. Fear and trust are pictured as an ongoing exchange, when I am afraid, I put my trust in You, modeling faith not as the absence of fear but as a repeated turning toward God when fear arises.
basic.originalLanguages: The verb batach, trust, anchors the psalm's refrain, an act of the will repeated three times amid real danger. David pictures his tears gathered into God's nod, bottle, and recorded in His book, a vivid image of God's intimate, personal attentiveness to every detail of suffering.
basic.typology: David's settled confidence amid mortal danger among hostile Philistines anticipates the greater confidence and obedience of Christ, who also faced hostile crowds and mortal danger yet entrusted Himself fully to the Father, and whose sufferings are likewise perfectly known and recorded by God.
basic.christ: The psalm's refrain, in God I trust, I shall not be afraid, what can man do to me, is quoted directly in Hebrews 13:6 as a settled confession for every believer under the new covenant. David's confidence that his tears and wanderings are known intimately by God anticipates the fuller promise that God will wipe away every tear from His people's eyes in the new creation, Revelation 21:4, a comfort secured entirely through Christ, who Himself faced hostile crowds, false accusation, and mortal danger, yet entrusted Himself completely to the Father.
basic.god: God is shown as intimately attentive to the smallest details of suffering, counting wanderings and collecting tears, while also being the sovereign one before whom human threats amount to nothing, what can man do to me, when God is trusted.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 21:10-15 records the historical event, David seized by the Philistines in Gath. Hebrews 13:6 quotes this psalm's refrain directly as the church's own confession of confidence in God. Revelation 21:4 promises that God will wipe away every tear, the fuller answer to this psalm's picture of tears gathered in God's bottle.
basic.oneStory: Seized by hostile enemies, David turns fear into a settled trust, repeated three times, in God I trust, I shall not be afraid. That same confession becomes the church's own in Hebrews 13:6, grounded fully in Christ, who faced mortal danger and entrusted Himself to the Father who now gathers every tear of His people toward the day He wipes them all away.

context.literary: A lament psalm structured around a threefold refrain, verses 3-4, 10-11, expressing trust that overcomes fear, framing an account of enemies' scheming, verses 5-7, and God's intimate care, verses 8-9, before closing with a vow of thanksgiving, verses 12-13.
context.historical: The heading ties this psalm to David's capture by the Philistines in Gath, 1 Samuel 21:10-15, when he feigned madness to escape with his life, one of the most physically precarious moments of his flight from Saul.
context.canonical: Its refrain is picked up directly by the writer of Hebrews, 13:6, applying David's specific historical confidence to the ongoing life of the whole church under the new covenant.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model turning fear into deliberate trust in God amid real, mortal danger, and to express confidence that God's intimate knowledge of suffering, every tear and every wandering step, secures ultimate deliverance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific historical danger among the Philistines; it prescribes an enduring pattern of faith for every believer, deliberately choosing trust over fear and finding comfort in God's intimate attentiveness to suffering.

debates.question: Does verse 7, 'cast down the nations,' express personal vengeance or an appeal to God's justice?

views.label: An appeal to God's own justice against real, ongoing hostility
views.case: David's prayer follows directly after describing enemies actively conspiring to take his life, verses 5-6, making this a plea for God's righteous intervention against genuine, unrepentant evil, not a personal vendetta David intends to carry out himself.

views.label: An honest, Spirit-inspired cry within the wider psalm's overall trajectory of trust
views.case: The verse sits within a psalm whose dominant note is trust rather than vengeance, and its harsh request is immediately followed by tender language of God's intimate care, verse 8, suggesting the imprecation is subordinate to, and resolved within, an overall posture of trust in God's justice rather than personal retaliation.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the psalm entrusts final judgment to God rather than to David's own hand, consistent with the New Testament's teaching to leave room for God's wrath, Romans 12:19.

study.originalLanguages: The verb batach, trust, anchors the psalm's refrain, an act of the will repeated across the psalm amid real danger. David pictures his tears gathered into God's nod, bottle, and recorded in His book, an image of God's intimate, personal attentiveness to every detail of his suffering.

typology.type: David's settled trust amid mortal danger among hostile enemies, refrain in verses 3-4, 10-11
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who faced hostile crowds and mortal danger yet fully entrusted Himself to the Father, 1 Peter 2:23, a trust the church now confesses in His name, Hebrews 13:6.

study.christ: The psalm's refrain, in God I trust, I shall not be afraid, what can man do to me, is quoted directly in Hebrews 13:6 as the church's own confession under the new covenant. David's confidence that his tears and wanderings are intimately known by God anticipates the fuller promise that God will wipe away every tear from His people's eyes in the new creation, Revelation 21:4, a comfort secured through Christ, who Himself faced hostile crowds and mortal danger yet entrusted Himself completely to the Father.
study.god: God is intimately attentive to the smallest details of suffering, counting wanderings and collecting tears, while remaining the sovereign one before whom human threats amount to nothing when He is trusted.

crossReferences.note: The historical account of David seized by the Philistines in Gath, the setting behind this psalm.
Quotes this psalm's refrain directly as the church's own confident confession.
God will wipe away every tear, the fuller answer to this psalm's picture of tears gathered in God's bottle.

study.oneStory: Seized by hostile enemies, David turns fear into settled trust, repeated as a refrain, in God I trust, I shall not be afraid. That same confession becomes the church's own in Hebrews 13:6, grounded fully in Christ, who faced mortal danger and entrusted Himself to the Father, who now gathers every tear of His people toward the day He wipes them all away.

quiz.q: What historical event does this psalm's heading connect it to?

options.text: David being seized by the Philistines in Gath while fleeing Saul.
options.note: This matches 1 Samuel 21:10-15, referenced in the psalm's heading.

options.text: David's coronation as king over all Israel.
options.note: This psalm's setting is a moment of acute danger, not a coronation.

options.text: The Ziphites betraying David's location to Saul.
options.note: That is the background for Psalm 54; this psalm's heading names David's capture in Gath instead.

options.text: David's battle against the Ammonites.
options.note: The heading specifically names Gath and the Philistines, not the Ammonites.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's central refrain, repeated in verses 4 and 11?

options.text: In God I trust, I will not be afraid; what can man do to me?
options.note: This refrain anchors the whole psalm and is quoted directly in Hebrews 13:6.

options.text: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want.
options.note: That refrain belongs to Psalm 23, not Psalm 56.

options.text: Cast your burden upon the Lord.
options.note: That is the key line of Psalm 55, not this psalm's refrain.

options.text: Be exalted, O God, above the heavens.
options.note: That refrain belongs to Psalm 57, not Psalm 56.

quiz.q: What image does David use in verse 8 to describe God's care for his suffering?

options.text: God gathering his tears into a bottle and recording his wanderings in a book.
options.note: This vivid image expresses God's intimate, detailed attentiveness to David's suffering.

options.text: God building him a physical fortress of stone.
options.note: Fortress imagery appears in other psalms, but verse 8's specific image here is tears and a book of record.

options.text: God sending an army of angels to fight visibly.
options.note: No such image appears in this verse; the image given is intimate and personal, not military.

options.text: God erasing all memory of David's suffering.
options.note: The opposite is true; God is pictured as carefully recording, not erasing, the record of David's suffering.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament use this psalm's refrain?

options.text: Hebrews 13:6 quotes it directly as the church's own confident confession before God.
options.note: This directly applies David's historical confidence to the ongoing life of every believer.

options.text: It is quoted to prove that fear is always sinful and forbidden.
options.note: The psalm itself acknowledges real fear, verse 3, and models turning to trust rather than denying fear exists.

options.text: It is used only as a historical example with no present application.
options.note: Hebrews 13:6 applies the refrain directly and presently to believers, not merely as distant history.

options.text: It is quoted to argue believers should avoid all human relationships.
options.note: This is unrelated to the refrain's actual meaning, which concerns fear of harm, not withdrawal from relationships.

quiz.q: What does David vow to do in verses 12-13 once deliverance comes?

options.text: Render thank offerings and walk before God in the light of life.
options.note: This closing vow of gratitude and continued faithful living concludes the psalm.

options.text: Retire from public life entirely.
options.note: No such vow appears; David vows thanksgiving and continued walking before God.

options.text: Seek personal revenge against the Philistines who seized him.
options.note: The psalm leaves judgment to God, verse 7, rather than vowing personal revenge.

options.text: Never again feel afraid in any circumstance.
options.note: The psalm's pattern is honest acknowledgment of fear met by deliberate trust, not a promise of never feeling fear again.

questions: David admits plainly, 'When I am afraid, I put my trust in You,' treating fear and trust as a real, ongoing exchange rather than pretending fear does not exist. What fear are you currently facing, and what would it look like to consciously turn it into trust today?

David finds comfort in knowing God counts his tears and records his wanderings. How does knowing God is intimately, personally aware of your specific struggles change how you bring them to Him in prayer?

The refrain 'what can man do to me' does not deny that people can cause real harm, but reframes their power in light of God's sovereignty. Where do you need this same reframing regarding someone or something that currently intimidates you?

Hebrews 13:6 takes David's specific historical confession and applies it to every believer. How does knowing this confidence is meant for you personally, not just for David, change how you read this psalm?
David vows to walk before God in the light of life once delivered. What would walking faithfully before God, in gratitude, look like for you in the situation you are currently facing?

translationNotes.note: Put my tears in Your bottle draws on the ancient Near Eastern practice of small tear vessels used in mourning; the image conveys that not one tear of genuine suffering is lost on God, but carefully kept and remembered.
By this I will know that God is on my side ties David's assurance directly to God's promised deliverance, an assurance grounded in God's past faithfulness rather than in present circumstances alone.
Walk before God in the light of life is a Hebrew idiom for living faithfully in ongoing fellowship with God, contrasted with the darkness of death from which God has delivered him.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 56:1-13. Em dashes present in the raw BSB text at verses 4 and 8 were converted to commas. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) confirmed for Psalm 56 this session; sermon status set to none.
